verses.text: The LORD: when a man makes a singular vow, valuations: male 20-60 — fifty shekels. Female — thirty. Male 5-20 — twenty; female — ten. Male one month to five — five; female — three. Male over sixty — fifteen; female — ten. If poorer, priest values by ability. Guidance: A vow could dedicate a person — yourself or a child — to God. The priest sets a price the household can pay. Even the vow is means-tested. Faithfulness shouldn't bankrupt the family it was made in.

If a beast vowed is one that may be offered — holy, not changed (good for bad, bad for good); if changed, both holy. If an unclean beast not offered — bring to priest, valued; if redeem, add a fifth. Guidance: Once you've offered something to God, don't swap it. Even if the new animal is better, the trade itself profanes both. God doesn't bargain. The fifth-added penalty stops the second-guessing economy at the altar door.

If a man sanctify his house to be holy unto the LORD — priest values, good or bad; as the priest values, so it stands. If he redeem, add a fifth — and it shall be his. Guidance: Even your house can be vowed. The priest appraises it; if you change your mind, the buy-back has a twenty percent premium. God values the gesture but charges interest on the reversal.

If a man sanctify a field of his possession — value by years to Jubilee. From Jubilee: full price; after, deduct. If redeem, add a fifth. If not redeem, or sold to another — not redeemable. In Jubilee — holy to the LORD, priest's possession. Guidance: Vowed inherited land is priced by harvest seasons left. Sell it on, and you lose the redemption right; it ends up the priest's at Jubilee. God's worth-counting always remembers the calendar.

If he sanctify a field bought, not of his inheritance — count to Jubilee for value, give the offering. In Jubilee the field returns to him from whom it was bought. All valuations by the sanctuary shekel — twenty gerahs. Guidance: A leased field returns to its owner at Jubilee — only the years between count. God's economy isn't punitive but proportional. One scale for all valuation: the sanctuary shekel. The yardstick belongs to the temple.

Firstling of beasts — already the LORD's; not sanctified again. If unclean — redeem with valuation plus a fifth. Devoted thing (cherem) by man — not sold, not redeemed; most holy. None devoted of men shall be redeemed — put to death. Guidance: Some things are utterly God's — cherem, the devoted ones. Once dedicated, never released. The terrible note about devoted persons refers to judicial execution under the law's harshest cases; it never permits human sacrifice.

All tithe of the land — seed and fruit — the LORD's, holy. If redeem the tithe, add a fifth. Tithe of herd or flock — every tenth that passes under the rod is holy. Search not good or bad, not change; if changed, both holy. Guidance: God gets the tenth — every tenth animal under the rod, no cherry-picking. The herder doesn't choose; the count decides. Trust gives up the right to pick the lamb. The tenth that walks under is His.

These are the commandments which the LORD commanded Moses for the children of Israel in mount Sinai. Guidance: Leviticus closes the way it lived — at Sinai, with God's voice from the tent. Three full books of Torah have unfolded since the burning bush. The next page, Numbers, opens with a census; the camp is finally ready to move.
